content.text: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard published by Anthropic that defines how AI applications connect to external tools, data sources, and services. It separates the
(the AI application that runs the model) from the
(the process that owns a capability), with a well-defined JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol between them.

MCP solves the N×M integration problem: without it, every AI application must write a custom adapter for every service it uses. With MCP, any MCP-capable host can use any MCP server — one interface on each side instead of N×M custom bridges.

Before MCP, integrating a service into an AI agent meant writing a custom tool: defining the schema, handling auth, making HTTP calls, parsing responses, and managing errors — repeated for every service. Multiply that by dozens of services and multiple AI frameworks, and the maintenance cost is enormous.
— JSON-RPC 2.0 messages with a fixed set of methods (
— stdio for local servers, HTTP+SSE or WebSocket for remote ones.
— Tools (callable functions), Resources (readable data), Prompts (reusable templates), and Sampling (server-initiated model calls).
— initialize → advertise capabilities → exchange messages → close.
With this standard, a GitHub MCP server written once in TypeScript works in Claude Desktop, Claude Code, any Python agent using the
SDK, or any other MCP-capable host — no per-host modifications needed.
MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 as its envelope format. Every request has a
handshake where client and server negotiate the protocol version and capabilities:
After initialization, the client lists tools and calls them using two methods:
Building an MCP Client in Python
) handles JSON-RPC serialization, but a minimal client is easy to build directly over subprocess stdio — which is useful for understanding the protocol:
Wire the client into a standard Claude tool-use loop. The
parameter — the schema returned by MCP is already in the Claude-compatible format:

Beyond callable tools, MCP servers can expose Resources — read-only data items addressed by URI. Resources are appropriate for files, database rows, or API snapshots that the model should read but not modify:
. Unlike tools, resources do not execute side effects — they return content blocks that the model can include in context.
MCP's security model is straightforward: the server owns credentials, not the model. A GitHub MCP server holds the GitHub API token in its own process environment. The model issues a
The server holds API keys and OAuth tokens in its own environment. The model issues a
request with structured arguments; the server injects auth before calling the upstream API. Credentials never appear in the JSON-RPC messages the model sees.
: each MCP server only has access to the credentials it needs. A Slack server cannot accidentally use your GitHub token.
: because all calls go through the server, you can log every tool invocation at one point rather than scattering auth and logging across multiple tool implementations.
: run separate MCP server processes for different sensitivity levels (e.g. read-only DB server vs. write-capable DB server).
Using the Official MCP Python SDK
The mcp package provides async context managers that handle connection setup, the initialize handshake, and message framing automatically:

# Install the official MCP Python SDK
# pip install mcp

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def list_mcp_tools(server_command: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    """Connect to an MCP server and return Claude-ready tool schemas."""
    server = StdioServerParameters(command=server_command[0], args=server_command[1:])
    async with stdio_client(server) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools_result = await session.list_tools()
            return [
                {
                    "name": tool.name,
                    "description": tool.description or "",
                    "input_schema": tool.inputSchema,
                }
                for tool in tools_result.tools
            ]

async def call_mcp_tool_sdk(server_command: list[str], name: str, args: dict) -> str:
    server = StdioServerParameters(command=server_command[0], args=server_command[1:])
    async with stdio_client(server) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool(name, args)
            return '\n'.join(
                c.text for c in result.content if hasattr(c, 'text')
            )

# Run with:
# asyncio.run(list_mcp_tools(['npx', '-y', '@modelcontextprotocol/server-github']))
The SDK abstracts the raw JSON-RPC messages shown in the previous sections. Use it in production; use the raw subprocess client only when debugging protocol behavior.
Stdio works for local processes. For hosted MCP services (servers running on a remote machine or as a cloud function), MCP defines an HTTP + Server-Sent Events transport:

# HTTP+SSE transport: connect to a remote MCP server over HTTPS
# Useful when the server runs as a hosted service, not a local subprocess

from mcp.client.sse import sse_client
from mcp import ClientSession
import httpx

async def connect_remote_mcp(url: str, api_key: str) -> list[dict]:
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"}
    async with sse_client(url, headers=headers) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools_result = await session.list_tools()
            return [
                {
                    "name": t.name,
                    "description": t.description or "",
                    "input_schema": t.inputSchema,
                }
                for t in tools_result.tools
            ]

# The URL format follows the server's deployment:
# await connect_remote_mcp('https://mcp.example.com/sse', os.environ['MCP_API_KEY'])
: HTTP POST for each JSON-RPC request
: SSE stream for responses and server-initiated notifications
: standard HTTP headers (Bearer token, API key) — the MCP protocol itself is auth-agnostic
The Prompts primitive lets a server expose parameterized prompt templates. The host fills in arguments, and the server returns fully-formed message content ready to inject into the conversation:

Prompts differ from tools in that they return message content, not tool results. They are useful for standardizing how agents approach recurring tasks (e.g. always summarizing PRs in the same structured format).
MCP is an open standard for connecting AI applications to external tools and data, using JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, HTTP, or WebSocket.
It solves the N×M integration problem: one MCP server works with any MCP-capable host.
The core primitives are Tools (callable functions) and Resources (readable data), discovered dynamically via
The protocol lifecycle is: initialize → capabilities → call → close.
Credentials stay in the server process; the model never sees API keys or tokens.
) or implement the JSON-RPC wire protocol directly for full control.
